# gg-walker-wasm

Tier-C "interpreter-embed" walker for the Betti → WASM pipeline: one
program-independent wasm32 module that executes the **property-carried
subset** of GG orchestration topologies and TRAPS fail-closed on everything
else. Host lowering (tree-sitter, all ~20 languages) stays in
`gnosis-polyglot`; only the walk crosses into wasm.

## How it composes

- `gnosis_polyglot::parse_and_extract_orchestration` lowers source to
  `GgTopology` **at build time on the host**.
- `gnosis_polyglot::aot_gg_embed::emit_gg_embed_for_export` packages THIS
  crate's prebuilt cdylib + a `gnosis-betti-manifest` custom section
  (`tier: "interpreter-embed"`, `certified_class: "gg-embed"`), plus a
  `<artifactId>.topology.json` sidecar pinned by `topologyHash`.
- Hosts run the module through the gnosis json-v1 ABI (`memory`,
  `gnosis_alloc`, `main(ptr,len)`, `gnosis_result_len`, `gnosis_free`;
  zero imports) with the **envelope input** — v1
  `{"topology": …, "input": …}` or v2
  `{"topology": …, "helpers": {"<fn>": <GgTopology>}, "input": …}` (exactly
  those keys; see the crate docs for the normative contract). The sidecar is
  the envelope material: a bare topology (helper-free exports) or the
  `{"topology","helpers"}` wrapper (exports whose certified call graph
  reaches other extracted functions, e.g. `echo.ts`). The topology rides in
  the input, not a data segment — a cdylib cannot read its own custom
  sections; data-segment splicing is documented future work.

## Build

```sh
cargo build --target wasm32-unknown-unknown --release
# → target/wasm32-unknown-unknown/release/gg_walker_wasm.wasm (~200 KB)
```

The packager fails closed when this binary is missing; override the location
with `GG_WALKER_WASM_PATH`. The crate is deliberately standalone (own
`[workspace]`, like `open-source/swerve`) and is ALSO consumed as an rlib by
`gnosis-polyglot` so the emitter can run the walker's static admission sweep
natively at package time.

## v2 certified coverage (exact, verified against gnexec)

See the `src/lib.rs` module docs for the normative list. Summary:

- Entry / Return / Statement-call nodes with property-carried semantics;
  Entry `param_names` binds frames (root: at most one parameter, bound to
  the program input; helpers: positional binding to evaluated call args).
- The explicit foil `Promise.all` Fork/Fold pair with the certified branch
  builtins (`loadUser`/`loadProfile` over `input.userId`) and `merge`
  (`"|"` join); property-free `TsBridge*` pass-through chains.
- **User-defined helper calls** (new in v2): statement-level
  `callee`/`args`/`bind` resolved against the envelope's `helpers` map —
  args limited to `literal`/`ident`/`member` IR, arity checked against the
  helper's `param_names`, call graph must be ACYCLIC (recursion declines at
  package time and traps at run time), helper names shadowing the builtins
  decline. Helper frames see only their bound parameters (the legacy
  `input.<field>` string form declines inside helpers).
- `expr` forms: bare identifiers, `input.<field>` (root frame), quoted
  literals, and IR `literal` / `ident` / `member` (over a bound plain
  identifier only) / `template` (**string-only**: every interpolated part
  must evaluate to a JSON string; numbers/null trap where gnexec would
  stringify — decline, not diverge).

Everything else — labels, callees without a helper topology, edge types
(`Race`/`Vent`/…), `binary` arithmetic, loops, `call` in expression
position (e.g. fib.ts's `return String(value)`), deep member chains —
declines: `WalkError` natively, a real `unreachable` trap under wasm32
(`panic = "abort"`). fib.ts therefore still declines by design:
loops/arithmetic are Tier A's compiled lane, deliberately not built here.
Coverage grows by widening host-side extractor property coverage, not
wasm-side language support.

## envelope v3 — bounded-step walks + cursors (Wave C)

Two OPTIONAL envelope keys (v1/v2 stay byte-compatible; unknown keys still
decline): `budget` (a non-negative integer HOP budget) and `resume` (a
previously returned cursor). When the budget exhausts mid-walk, `main`
COMPLETES (no trap) with a JSON cursor

```json
{"__gg_cursor__": {"nodeId": …, "frames": [{"t", "nodeId", "scope", "pending"}…],
                   "pending": …, "ordinal": N, "topo": "<fnv1a64>"}}
```

— the serializable frame stack at a node boundary. `{…, "resume": <cursor>}`
continues the walk (same topology/helpers/input required; `topo`
fingerprints the program, so a cursor can never resume against a different
one — fail closed, like every malformed/tampered cursor). `ordinal` (total
hops consumed) is strictly monotone across suspensions — the replay anchor
of the stateful teleport lane
(`open-source/x-gnosis/docs/BETTI_STATEFUL_TELEPORT_DESIGN.md`). Native
coverage: `tests/cursor_resume.rs`; cross-substrate (swerve/wasmtime →
betti-station/v8): `open-source/swerve/tests/snapshot_resume_integration.rs`
and `open-source/x-gnosis/src/__tests__/betti-station-cursor-resume.test.ts`.

## Tests

```sh
cargo test   # native rlib walk + fail-closed suite
```

`tests/fixtures/*.topology.json` are checked-in extractions of
`gnode/benchmarks/{fanout.gg,echo.gg,echo.ts}` (this crate must not depend
on gnosis-polyglot, which cannot build for wasm32). Drift is pinned by
`polyglot/tests/gg_embed_correctness.rs`; regenerate after intentional
extractor changes with:

```sh
cd .. && cargo test --test gg_embed_correctness regen_walker_fixtures -- --ignored
```

Wasm-level parity vs gnexec (fanout → `ada:user|ada:profile`, echo.gg →
`null`, echo.ts → `hello:<name>` through the v2 helpers envelope) and trap
fail-closure run under Node in the same polyglot test file; native Tier-C
call/template/member trap coverage lives in `tests/tier_c_calls.rs`.
